title: Conditional Generative Models Enable Targeted Exploration of MAX Phase Design Space
authors: Jamie Swain, Cyprien Bone, Matthew T. Darby, Ewan Galloway, Keith T. Butler
abstract: MAX phases (M$_{n+1}$AX$_n$), precursors to MXenes, span a vast compositional space, motivating efficient computational screening for synthesisable candidates. We employ CrystaLLM$-π$, a large language model fine-tuned on 6,179 double transition-metal MAX phases, and demonstrate its ability to generate out-of-sample structures consistent with known experimental trends. Using a conditioning vector with two dimensions (a statistically derived MXene derivative count and a surrogate for A-site binding energy), the model was able to target MXene-favourable regions of phase space for generation. Specific condition vectors double novel stable structure generation rates versus unconditioned baselines. Of ten compositionally novel candidates, five exhibit DFT-validated stability ($E_{hull} < 0.050$ eV/atom). This work showcases the potential for autoregressive generative models to explore targeted materials' spaces, offering a scalable framework for accelerated discovery in compositionally complex systems.
summary: This paper presents a conditional generative large language model designed to accelerate the discovery of MAX phases and their MXene derivatives. By conditioning the model on physical and statistical properties, the researchers can target specific regions of the material design space to find novel, thermodynamically stable structures. This approach significantly reduces the need for expensive first-principles calculations during the initial screening process.
main_problem: Efficiently exploring the vast compositional and structural design space of MAX phases to identify stable, synthesizable candidates and precursors for MXenes without the computational bottleneck of traditional DFT screening.
main_result: The CrystaLLM-pi model can target specific regions of phase space using conditioning vectors, doubling the generation rate of novel stable structures, with 50% of novel candidates passing stringent DFT stability validation.
method: A multi-stage pipeline involving fine-tuning a transformer-based LLM on MAX phase CIFs, using property-based conditioning vectors, and validating results via MACE/ALIGNN machine-learned potentials and DFT.
model_or_system: The system consists of MAX phases (M_{n+1}AX_n) and MAB phases. The model is CrystaLLM-pi, an autoregressive generative large language model fine-tuned on 6,179 double transition-metal MAX phases.
key_observables: MXene derivative count, A-site well curvature (surrogate for vacancy formation energy), energy above hull (E_hull), and mechanical properties (bulk and shear moduli).
important_parameters: Stability threshold of E_hull < 0.050 eV/atom; conditioning vectors for MXene count (0.75-0.95) and A-site curvature (0.0-0.4).
main_assumptions: The model assumes that structural features encoded in CIFs can be effectively learned and that A-site well curvature serves as a valid proxy for exfoliation potential.
figures_summary: Fig 1: A-site sublattice perturbation schematic; Fig 2: Distribution of A-site well curvature; Fig 3: Correlation between condition vectors and calculated curvature; Fig 4: Comparison of generation rates; Fig 5: Element frequency and stability heatmap; Fig 6: Generative workflow.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the MAX phase design problem, details the CrystaLLM-pi architecture and conditioning mechanism, describes the multi-stage validation workflow, presents results for targeted generation and stability, and concludes with a low-shot analysis of MAB phases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of an A-site sublattice perturbation in a representative double transition metal MAX phase unit cell and the corresponding potential energy change. The A-site atom is shown in purple, transition metal M-site atoms in light blue and red, and X-site atoms in black.
FIG. 3. Plots showing the calculated curvature (arbitrary units) across a sweeping A-site well condition vector, with constant MXene derivative count dimension.
FIG. 2. Histogram displaying the min-max normalised A-site well curvature values against MAX phase count within the training dataset.

FIG. 4. Bar chart detailing novel stable structure gener- ation counts with respect to condition vector under non- compositional prompting. The dashed line indicates the non- conditional model baseline. Prompting performed within a condition vector space biased toward high potential MXene derivative counts and low A-site well curvature.
FIG. 5. Element frequency heatmap for the set of novel stable structures showing element usage weighted by stoichiometry (upper left corner) and the mean energy above hull (lower right corner).
